plain: An AI learns patterns from the examples it's shown, so if those examples are skewed, the system quietly absorbs and repeats the skew — while looking neutral and objective. The bias isn't in the code; it's in the diet.
analogy: A child raised hearing only one side of every story will repeat that side as obvious fact, never knowing there was another.
prompt: When a system gives you a 'neutral' recommendation, whose examples do you think it learned from?

hook: Most people never change the setting they were given.
plain: Whatever option is pre-selected becomes what the vast majority of people end up with, because changing it takes effort and most never bother. Whoever chooses the default quietly chooses for almost everyone.
analogy: Countries where organ donation is opt-out have donation rates above 90%; nearly identical countries where it's opt-in sit near 15% — same people, different default.
prompt: Which of your phone or account settings did you actually choose, versus just inherit because someone set it for you?

hook: They don't keep you because it's best — they keep you because leaving is painful.
plain: Once your photos, contacts, files, and habits live inside one company's system, moving elsewhere means losing or rebuilding all of it. The pain of leaving, not the quality of the product, is often what keeps you.
analogy: Staying in a flat you've outgrown because the thought of packing, moving, and re-setting-up everything is worse than just tolerating it.
prompt: Which service are you staying with purely because the hassle of leaving feels bigger than the annoyance of staying?

hook: How fast it starts and how much it carries are two different things.
plain: Bandwidth is how much data can move at once; latency is the delay before anything moves at all. A connection can have huge capacity yet still feel slow if every request takes a moment to begin.
analogy: A wide motorway moves thousands of cars (bandwidth), but if the toll gate takes 30 seconds per car before they even set off (latency), the journey still feels slow.
prompt: Where do people confuse 'we can handle a lot' with 'we respond quickly' — and treat fixing one as fixing the other?

hook: Every shortcut taken now is a bill that arrives later, with interest.
plain: When something is built quickly with cut corners, it works for now but becomes harder and harder to change later. The 'debt' is the future effort owed to fix what was rushed — and ignoring it makes it grow.
analogy: Stacking new boxes in front of the ones you need instead of organising the garage — fast today, but every future trip in there costs you more time.
prompt: Where is a quick fix from the past now silently making every new change slower than it should be?

hook: One part breaks and the whole thing goes down with it.
plain: A system has a single point of failure when one component, with no backup, can take everything down if it fails. Resilience comes from making sure nothing critical depends on just one thing.
analogy: A team where only one person knows the password to the main account — if they're unreachable, everyone is stuck, no matter how capable the rest are.
prompt: In a system you rely on, what one thing breaking would stop everything — and why is there no backup?

hook: If you can't be clever, try every possibility until one works.
plain: Brute force is solving a problem by exhaustively trying every option rather than finding a smart shortcut. It always works given enough time — which is exactly why the defence is making 'enough time' impossibly long.
analogy: A thief with no skill trying every number from 0000 to 9999 on a padlock — guaranteed to open it eventually, unless there are far too many combinations to get through.
prompt: Where does the safety of something you use depend not on it being unbreakable, but on breaking it taking too long to bother?

hook: Simple parts following simple rules can produce something nobody designed.
plain: Complex, intelligent-looking behaviour can arise from many small components each following basic rules, with no central planner. The whole does things none of the parts were programmed to do.
analogy: No single ant knows how to build a colony, yet thousands following tiny instinctive rules produce tunnels, farms, and defences — the design lives in the swarm, not any ant.
prompt: Where might a behaviour you assume was deliberately designed actually be an unplanned by-product of simple rules?

hook: Designed not to help you, but to trick you into what they want.
plain: Some interfaces are deliberately built to manipulate you — hiding the cancel button, pre-ticking boxes, making 'no' hard to find. It's not bad design; it's effective design aimed against your interests.
analogy: A shop where the exit is hidden behind a maze of displays you must walk through — you weren't lost, you were routed.
prompt: When did a screen recently make the choice you wanted hard and the choice they wanted easy — and did you give in?

hook: It isn't built to inform you — it's built to keep you.
plain: The systems behind most feeds optimise for one thing: time spent. Truth, balance, and your wellbeing are irrelevant to the goal unless they happen to increase engagement — which is why outrage and extremes rise to the top.
analogy: A casino has no clocks and no windows — not by accident, but because every design choice serves one aim: keeping you in the seat.
prompt: What does your most-used app actually optimise for — and is that the same as what's good for you?

hook: Government spends 45% of GDP but has no creative function — only press relations.
plain: Governments are the largest organizations in any economy, controlling nearly half of all spending, yet they have no marketing or creative function. Their only way to reach the public is through journalists, who are incentivized to make them look bad. This is like advertising a product exclusively through your harshest critics. If governments learned to communicate directly and frame policies as exchanges, public trust and compliance would soar.
analogy: A company launches a new product but only lets hostile bloggers announce it. No ads, no website, no controlled messaging. Predictably, the launch fails and everyone blames the product.
prompt: If you ran a government department, what policy currently framed as an imposition could you reframe as a fair trade to increase public buy-in?

hook: Do it first — justify it later.
plain: Most breakthroughs happen when someone does something, it works, and then they rationalize it afterward. Trying to build a rational framework before acting kills the idea. Ship the thing, measure the result, then write the deck explaining why you're a genius.
analogy: Share a Coke with names on cans didn't come from a 56-page PowerPoint on personalization. Someone had the idea, Coke tried it, it worked, and then everyone post-rationalized why it was brilliant.
prompt: What's one thing you've wanted to try but haven't because you can't justify it in advance? What would happen if you just did it and explained it later?

hook: Three tiers reduce choice anxiety — not 27 options.
plain: When people are overwhelmed, they don't want infinite choice — they want a clear tier system. Good, Better, Best gives them permission to pick based on budget and confidence, without agonizing over 27 nearly-identical products. This isn't about limiting options — it's about making the choice feel easy.
analogy: Costco limits what's on the shelf. You don't need a million types of bread — you just want one that's good enough. The constraint makes the decision trivial.
prompt: If you had to collapse your product line into three tiers — entry, standard, premium — what would each one be, and what would you name them so a stranger understands in 3 seconds?

hook: Adoption is slow, then explosive, then flat — most innovations die in the slow phase.
plain: New behaviors follow an S-shaped adoption curve: flat at the start while early adopters test it, steep in the middle as social proof kicks in, then flat again at saturation. Companies expect linear growth and kill innovations during the initial flat phase, before the steep part ever arrives. Google Glass, killed in 2014, would likely be ubiquitous by now if they'd held through the curve.
analogy: A gym opens and signs 20 members in month one, 25 in month two. Investors pull funding for 'underperformance.' Month seven would have signed 400 as word-of-mouth hit critical mass.
prompt: Which project or initiative in your organization is currently in the flat zone of the S-curve and at risk of being killed before it reaches the steep part?

hook: Make the thing first, sell it second — inverts the agency model completely.
plain: Traditional agencies wait for a client to commission work. But when production becomes cheap and fast (via AI or other tools), companies could create finished ads, products, or ideas on spec and sell them afterward. The Beatles didn't wait for someone to commission 'Yellow Submarine' — they made it and sold it. This flips the power dynamic: instead of begging for a brief, you arrive with the solution and name your price.
analogy: A production company creates ten brilliant 30-second ads for brands that haven't asked for them, then pitches the finished work. Three brands buy them immediately because the risk is gone.
prompt: If you created one finished piece of work on spec this week — no client, no brief, just your best idea — who would you pitch it to and why?

hook: Invent the unit of measurement to make the comparison obvious.
plain: Matthew Boulton and James Watt invented 'horsepower' not as a technical standard but as a marketing tool. Potential buyers couldn't evaluate steam engines against the alternatives, so Boulton created a term that made the math simple: 'This 25-horsepower engine replaces 75 horses because they work in shifts.' Suddenly, the value was undeniable. The metric wasn't neutral science — it was persuasion.
analogy: A software company selling automation creates a metric: 'hours saved per week.' Customers couldn't compare products before, but now they see '40 hours saved per month' and the ROI is obvious.
prompt: What comparison are your customers unable to make because no one has invented the right unit of measurement to simplify the decision?

hook: Dopamine spikes when reality beats prediction — or undershoots it.
plain: Your brain releases dopamine not when you get a reward, but when a reward is better than you predicted. If you expect juice and get juice, dopamine stays flat. If you expect juice and don't get it, dopamine crashes. The gap between prediction and reality drives emotion — not the reward itself.
analogy: Monkeys in a lab get juice when a light turns on. After a while, dopamine spikes when the light appears — not when they drink. If the light comes on but no juice follows, dopamine crashes below baseline. Surprise is the drug, not the juice.
prompt: Think of a service you use regularly. What could the provider do in the next interaction that would violate your prediction in a positive way — something you'd notice because you weren't expecting it?

hook: Brands reduce risk — not increase excitement.
plain: The first thing people use a brand for is to make sure they're not making a bad decision. It's not about passion or identity — it's about avoiding regret. In a world of overwhelming choice, a known brand is shorthand for 'this is good enough and I won't look stupid'.
analogy: You don't buy McDonald's because you love it. You buy it because you know exactly what you're getting, and you won't be disappointed. The brand is a hedge against a bad meal.
prompt: What's a recent purchase where you chose the brand you knew over a cheaper option you'd never heard of? What were you really insuring against?

hook: Automation creates a hunger for the human — every vector has an opposite.
plain: When one trend dominates, a counter-trend emerges as people seek what the trend eliminated. As retail became automated and impersonal, farmers' markets surged. As airports became massive and efficient, London City Airport became popular for being small and having 'hardly any shops.' The more extreme a trend, the stronger the reaction that restores balance. There are no pure trends — only vectors with opposites.
analogy: Streaming killed physical media, so vinyl records became premium collectibles. The more music became free and infinite, the more people paid $40 for one album on a heavy black disc.
prompt: What dominant trend in your industry has created an underserved counter-trend, and who is quietly serving that backlash?

hook: Best marketing ideas emerge from culture, not campaigns — they happen outside the department.
plain: Marketing exists in two forms: as a controlled department within a company (the zoo), and as a cultural phenomenon that happens everywhere customers interact with value (the ecosystem). The most influential marketing — like 'bottomless brunch' or the rebranding of the London Overground — often comes from outside formal marketing teams. Real marketing thinking shapes pricing, naming, service design, and language across the entire business.
analogy: Starbucks didn't invent the 'third place' concept in a marketing meeting. Store managers and baristas created it through behavior, and marketing later codified what was already happening.
prompt: What marketing idea in your company emerged organically from employees or customers, and how could you amplify it instead of trying to control it?

hook: Chicago says lower prices; Austria says explore possibilities — one optimizes, one creates.
plain: Chicago school economics says businesses exist to deliver what customers already want at the lowest possible price. Austrian economics says businesses are discovery mechanisms that explore where new value can be created. Chicago thinking leads to cost-cutting and commoditization; Austrian thinking leads to innovation and new categories. Most business schools teach Chicago, which is why marketing is systematically undervalued.
analogy: Chicago: 'Build a cheaper taxi.' Austrian: 'What if we reimagined urban transportation entirely?' One gives you Uber, the other gives you a slightly cheaper Yellow Cab.
prompt: If your business stopped optimizing for efficiency for six months and started exploring adjacent possibilities, what would you try first?

hook: Obsessing over how you got there limits where you can go.
plain: Organizations demand that every decision follow a logical, sequential process because it feels rigorous. But requiring that everything 'make sense in advance' limits you to exploring only the obvious 15-20% of the solution space. Penicillin, Viagra, and Darwin's theory of evolution all happened backward — the discovery came first, the explanation later. Process worship kills serendipity.
analogy: A company rejects a brilliant ad concept because it didn't emerge from the six-stage creative brief process. A competitor runs it as spec work, it goes viral, and they win the client.
prompt: What result has your team achieved that you couldn't have planned for through a formal process, and why do you still insist on that process for everything else?

hook: Celebrities' favorite orders simplify choice — and drive sales.
plain: McDonald's 'Famous Orders' campaign took a celebrity's actual McDonald's order and put it on the menu as a one-click option. Travis Scott, Mariah Carey, Stormzy. It reduced decision fatigue, created social proof, and made ordering feel like insider knowledge. A choice-reduction hack disguised as celebrity endorsement.
analogy: You open the McDonald's app overwhelmed. Then you see 'The Travis Scott': a Quarter Pounder with bacon, fries with BBQ sauce, and a Sprite. Done. You don't have to think — someone famous already solved it for you.
prompt: If you could create a 'Famous Order' for your product — a pre-configured bundle associated with someone your customers admire — who would it be, and what would the bundle include?

hook: More options make confident decisions harder — not easier.
plain: When you're exposed to too many options, you can't be confident you've made the best decision. The risk of regret grows with every additional choice. This is why searching through page 3 of toasters or endlessly swiping on Tinder feels exhausting — you're no longer choosing, you're trying to avoid regret.
analogy: If you walk into a shop with 7 types of jam, you'll probably buy one. If there are 27, you'll leave without buying anything — not because you don't want jam, but because choosing feels impossible.
prompt: Name a purchase you've been delaying. How many options are you currently comparing — and what would happen if you cut that number to three?

hook: When a decision is too complex to calculate, compress it into a simple rule.
plain: Some decisions involve too many variables to process rationally, so experts create shortcut rules that approximate the right answer. 'Charging at 50 kW means you gain 50 miles per 20 minutes' is a heuristic for navigating electric vehicle charging without doing the math every time. Heuristics sacrifice precision for usability, making complex systems navigable.
analogy: A doctor treating chest pain doesn't recalculate cardiac risk from scratch. They use the rule: 'If two of these five symptoms are present, run the full test.' Faster, reliable, good enough.
prompt: What recurring complex decision in your work could be compressed into a one-sentence rule that's 90% accurate?

hook: 10% of marketing delivers 95% of the value — unpredictably.
plain: Most marketing efforts produce modest, linear results. But a tiny fraction — one campaign, one slogan, one product tweak — produces exponential returns that pay for everything else. You can't predict which 10% will be the winner, so optimizing everything for efficiency kills your chance of hitting the outlier.
analogy: In Hollywood, most films lose money. But one blockbuster pays for the 20 flops. If you only greenlit 'safe bets', you'd never make the one that prints billions.
prompt: Look at your last 10 marketing initiatives. Which one delivered results disproportionate to its budget — and did you try to repeat that structure, or did you just move on?

hook: People judge experience against prediction — not absolute quality.
plain: Your brain doesn't evaluate experiences in isolation — it compares them to what you expected. Ryanair gets better reviews than British Airways not because the service is objectively better, but because expectations are so low that anything acceptable feels like a win. Managing expectations before the experience is as important as the experience itself.
analogy: The only reason someone doesn't fly Ryanair is the 24 hours of dread beforehand — terror that your bag will be 1mm oversized and you'll be pistol-whipped by Serbian mercenaries. The actual flight? Perfectly fine. The pre-experience anxiety kills it.
prompt: What's one place in your customer journey where anxiety or inflated expectations ruin an otherwise decent experience? How could you reset predictions beforehand?

hook: What works for startups destroys value at scale.
plain: Performance marketing, real-time optimization, and personalized ads are brilliant for small companies with limited budgets. But when Coca-Cola or Unilever copy that playbook, they're abandoning what actually works at scale: mass reach, memorability, and brand-building. The tactics that made Shopify successful don't apply to P&G.
analogy: If you're a local landscaper selling hedge-trimming, Facebook ads targeting 'privacy hedge' keywords are magic. If you're Unilever, trying to have a 'one-on-one conversation' with billions of people is insane — you should be buying Super Bowl ads.
prompt: What digital tactic did you adopt because it seemed 'sophisticated' — even though your business model is closer to a 1970s brand than a 2015 startup?

hook: Calling back a missed call amazes customers — almost no one does it.
plain: Caller ID has existed for 20 years. Almost no business uses it to call back missed calls. When someone does, it creates disproportionate delight because it's so rare. This is a zero-cost way to surprise customers — and almost every business ignores it.
analogy: You call a Lotus dealership. No answer. Ten minutes later, they call you back: 'I was outside with a customer and saw I missed your call.' You're stunned. It's the second time in 20 years a business has done this.
prompt: How many missed calls does your business get per week? Who currently owns calling them back? If no one does, make it someone's job starting Monday.

hook: What arrives as choice becomes mandatory — hostility grows when convenience turns compulsory.
plain: A technology enters the market as a convenient alternative to the old way, then gradually becomes the only way. Parking apps started as optional shortcuts but became the only payment method, excluding people without smartphones. The shift from option to obligation alienates users who never consented to full dependence on the new system.
analogy: Self-checkout lanes at the supermarket were optional speed lanes. Now entire stores have removed human cashiers, forcing every customer — including the elderly or tech-averse — through the machines.
prompt: Which convenience feature in your product or service has silently become mandatory, and what friction is that creating for a segment of your users?

hook: Optimizing for efficiency kills value you never quantified — what can't be measured gets trampled.
plain: When organizations pursue efficiency above all, they systematically destroy benefits that were never captured in a spreadsheet. A ticket machine saves labor costs, but eliminates the trust-building moment when a clerk recommends a cheaper fare. The unmeasured value — relationship, serendipity, human discretion — gets discarded because it never appeared in the cost-benefit analysis.
analogy: A railway station replaces ticket clerks with machines to save wages. Passengers lose the clerk who would spot that buying a ticket from the next station saves £15 — a service that built loyalty but was invisible to finance.
prompt: What unmeasured benefit does your business provide that a pure efficiency audit would eliminate?

hook: Exploitation funds exploration — exploration directs what to exploit.
plain: You need two modes: exploitation (squeezing value from what you know works) and exploration (discovering new opportunities). They're not opposites — they're complementary. Exploit gives you cash flow to fund exploration; exploration tells you where to exploit next. Optimizing only for efficiency kills discovery.
analogy: AI researchers understand this instinctively. You can't build a great model by only exploiting known patterns — you need to explore edge cases and anomalies to find breakthroughs. Then you exploit what you learned.
prompt: What percentage of your budget or time last quarter went to exploration versus exploitation? If it was 100% exploit, name one thing you'll explore this month.

hook: Small annoying things fall between stools — someone should own them.
plain: Most organizations have no one responsible for fixing tiny irritations that fall between departments. The toaster button, the missed callback, the confusing menu — these create disproportionate value when solved, but no job title covers them. Agencies should pitch 'director of trivia' as a role: someone who fixes the little pissy things that really matter.
analogy: A Lotus dealership calls back a missed call 10 minutes later — the only time in 20 years a business has done that. Caller ID has existed for decades. Who owns that gap? No one. That's why it never gets fixed.
prompt: List three tiny, recurring irritations in your product or service. Who in your org currently owns fixing them? If the answer is 'no one', that's the problem.

hook: Concentrated proximity beats broad distribution — five neighbors trump fifteen strangers.
plain: People adopt new behaviors through observation of those nearby, not through abstract persuasion. Selling five solar panels to five households on the same street creates more future sales than fifteen scattered installations, because visibility and peer validation drive uptake. Humans are wired to copy what's common in their immediate environment, not what's rational in a brochure.
analogy: You install electric vehicle chargers in one office building, not ten scattered locations. Six months later, half the building drives EVs because colleagues saw it work and talked about it.
prompt: If you mapped your customer base geographically or socially, where is density highest — and are you leveraging that cluster to drive adoption in adjacent circles?

hook: More ways to buy equals more total buyers — reducing channels optimizes cost, kills sales.
plain: Customers have different preferences for how they want to purchase. Some want to call, some want to book online, some want to walk in and ask questions. Each additional channel you offer increases total conversions because you're removing friction for a different segment. Finance teams want to force everyone into the lowest-cost channel, which saves pennies per transaction but loses customers who needed a different path.
analogy: A restaurant removes phone orders and forces all reservations through an app. Tech-savvy diners love it; older customers who would've called and spent more just book elsewhere.
prompt: Which sales or service channel did your business eliminate in the last three years, and which customer segment quietly left with it?

hook: Identical products perform differently based on the badge — your brain writes the review.
plain: When Toyota and GM built the exact same car on the same production line and badged them differently, the Toyota had fewer customer complaints and higher resale value. Same car, same faults, different perception. Customers expected the Toyota to be reliable, so they interpreted the experience through that lens. Brand perception doesn't just influence purchase — it literally changes how you experience the product.
analogy: You drink wine from a £10 bottle and a £50 bottle. The £50 one tastes better. Later you learn they were the same wine in different bottles — but your memory insists the expensive one was superior.
prompt: If you secretly swapped the branding on your product with a luxury competitor's, how would your customers' experience and complaints change?

hook: One-click default orders eliminate decision fatigue at peak stress.
plain: A panic button is a product feature that saves your most common order and lets you reorder it with zero decisions. Domino's could do this: you open your fridge, find nothing, and hit a button pre-loaded with your favorite pizza. It removes friction at the exact moment decision-making is hardest.
analogy: Amazon Dash buttons for home essentials — one push and toilet paper arrives. The button exists because deciding which toilet paper to buy, every time, is exhausting.
prompt: What's the most repeated decision your customers make? How could you collapse that decision into a single button or saved default they activate under stress?

hook: Rational surprise is an oxymoron — just do something weird.
plain: You can't engineer surprise through a 56-page PowerPoint deck. Surprise works because it's subjective, arbitrary, and breaks pattern. The AO bear works because a founder decided to keep bears in the van — not because they AB-tested bears vs. aardvarks. Trying to rationalize surprise kills it.
analogy: A PLC trying to innovate: 'We need a framework for surprise definition. Can you prove the bear is better than a stuffed aardvark?' A founder just throws bears in the van.
prompt: What's one thing you could do this week that would surprise your customers, where the only justification is 'it felt right'?

hook: Technical people optimize for function; customers optimize for feeling — the gap is profit.
plain: Engineers and data-driven thinkers assume customers buy the objectively best product. But humans buy things that feel right, look impressive, or signal identity. A Toyota and a Chevrolet built on the same production line have different resale values purely because of brand perception. Engineering can't see this gap, which is why technical founders often undervalue marketing.
analogy: Two watches: one keeps time to within a millisecond, the other loses two minutes a day. The second one costs ten times more because it's a Rolex, and people buy the story, not the accuracy.
prompt: What attribute of your product does your team obsess over that customers barely notice, and what attribute do customers care about that you've never measured?

hook: Business uses addition when it needs sigmoid curves — adoption isn't linear, it's social.
plain: Most business planning assumes linear returns: double the input, double the output. But human adoption follows sigmoid curves — slow at first, explosive in the middle, plateauing at saturation. Products fail because companies expect instant traction and kill them before the social copying phase kicks in. Google Glass was ahead by 15 years; most innovations need patience through the steep part of the S-curve.
analogy: A new product sells five units in month one, seven in month two. Finance kills it for underperformance. Had they waited, month six would have sold 500 as early adopters made it safe for everyone else.
prompt: Which initiative in your business did you or your team abandon during the flat part of the adoption curve before social proof could kick in?

hook: We're biological creatures forced to navigate a system designed by engineers who hate ambiguity.
plain: Human beings are messy, emotional, and analog. But the digital systems we're forced to use every day are designed by people who think in binaries and optimize for efficiency. The result is friction: self-checkout machines that yell 'unidentified object in bagging area,' parking apps that exclude the elderly, and hotel showers with no soap. We're being forced to adapt to machines instead of machines adapting to us.
analogy: You're asked to authorize a transaction on your phone, but your phone is in another room charging. The system assumes you carry it everywhere — you just want to finish your work.
prompt: What digital system in your life forces you to behave in a way that feels unnatural, and how would you redesign it to fit human behavior instead?

hook: Breakthrough campaigns came from watching the thing being made.
plain: In the 2000s, ad agencies sent creatives on factory tours, supplier meetings, and retailer conventions. That's where the best insights came from — seeing how the product was actually made, who bought it, and why. When agencies stopped doing factory tours, they lost the source of their best ideas.
analogy: The Four Springs Butter campaign — one of the most famous in UK advertising — came from a factory tour. Someone noticed something in how the butter was made and built a campaign around it. You can't get that from a Zoom briefing.
prompt: When's the last time you spent a full day in the environment where your product is made, sold, or used? If it's been more than 6 months, book one this quarter.

hook: Not knowing when something will happen feels worse than waiting longer.
plain: People tolerate long waits if they know how long. They can't tolerate uncertainty, even over short periods. Uber didn't win by making cabs faster — it won by showing you exactly where your car is and when it will arrive. An insurance company that promises payment in four weeks and delivers in two gets thank-you calls; one that delivers in two weeks with no timeline gets complaints.
analogy: You're stuck in a traffic jam with no information. Twenty minutes feels unbearable. Same jam, but a sign says '15-minute delay due to accident ahead' — suddenly it's tolerable.
prompt: Where in your customer's experience are they waiting without knowing how long, and could you fix their anxiety just by giving them a timeline?

term: Marketing = How You Think, Not What You Do
hook: Marketing isn't a department — it's a worldview that value is created in minds, not factories.
plain: Most businesses treat marketing as the team that makes ads. But marketing as a philosophy means understanding that perception, framing, and customer experience create value independent of the physical product. The biggest marketing ideas — like 'bottomless brunch' or renaming a railway line to sound like a subway — often come from outside marketing departments because the thinking applies everywhere.
analogy: The London Overground was a failing railway until someone rebranded it to sound like the Tube. Usage soared with zero infrastructure change — pure perception shift.
prompt: If your company stopped spending on ads tomorrow but kept applying marketing thinking to pricing, naming, and customer experience, what would change first?

hook: Solving the fear is often cheaper than solving the fact — perception beats performance.
plain: When customers express concern about a product limitation, you can address the limitation itself or address the worry. Range anxiety in electric cars can be solved by building bigger batteries or by reducing the uncertainty around charging. The second is cheaper and often more effective because the anxiety, not the actual range, is what stops purchase.
analogy: An insurance company spends millions speeding up claims processing. Alternatively, they send a letter saying 'expect payment within four weeks' and deliver in two — complaints stop, costs stay flat.
prompt: What customer complaint in your business is actually a fear or uncertainty that could be solved with better communication instead of better engineering?

hook: Most Advanced Yet Acceptable — too far ahead and adoption dies.
plain: Coined by industrial designer Raymond Loewy, MAYA describes the sweet spot where a product is innovative enough to be interesting but familiar enough to be comfortable. Google Glass failed because it was Most Advanced, Not Acceptable — too alien, no social proof, immediate stigma. Ray-Ban Meta glasses succeed because they look like normal glasses, making the tech adoption frictionless.
analogy: A car company designs a concept vehicle with no steering wheel and butterfly doors. Cool, but unsellable. They add back the steering wheel, keep the doors normal, and suddenly it's a hit.
prompt: What feature in your product is too advanced for your audience to accept right now, and how could you phase it in behind something familiar?

hook: Change how you sell — not what you sell.
plain: Commercial innovation means innovating on pricing, presentation, packaging, or distribution without changing the core product. 'Share a Coke' didn't invent a new drink — it put names on cans. Amazon Prime didn't invent new products — it changed how you pay and receive them. Often, these shifts create more value than product R&D.
analogy: Selling shampoo in tiny bottles at airports for triple the per-ounce price isn't product innovation — it's commercial innovation. You're solving a different job: 'I need shampoo but I'm leaving tomorrow and don't want a full bottle'.
prompt: Pick your core product. How could you repackage, reprice, or redistribute it to solve a different customer job without changing what's inside?

hook: Brand returns compound over time — judging by linear metrics guarantees underinvestment.
plain: Money spent on brand-building doesn't deliver returns in straight lines — it accumulates. Early investment looks inefficient, but after a threshold, awareness and trust begin multiplying each other. Finance systems judge brand work as if every dollar should produce immediate sales, which systematically starves long-term equity. Short-term thinking rewards the person who inherits the brand you built, not you.
analogy: You invest in reputation for five years while competitors chase quarterly sales. Year six, customers choose you by default because you're the one they trust — your competitor's lead evaporates overnight.
prompt: If you were forced to choose between a 10% revenue lift this quarter or building brand equity that pays off in three years, which does your incentive structure actually reward?

hook: Founder-led companies outperform management-led peers — consistently.
plain: Companies where the founder still runs the business consistently outperform those run by professional managers. Founders have frontline focus (they talk to customers), bias for action (they ship fast), and an owner's mentality (they treat every dollar like their own). PLCs optimize for quarterly EBITDA; founders optimize for long-term customer loyalty.
analogy: Willie Walsh, CEO of British Airways' parent company, used to be a pilot. He spent time in front of the mast. Contrast that with a finance MBA parachuted in from McKinsey — the mental models are completely different.
prompt: If you ran your company like you owned 100% of it with your own money on the line, what's the first decision you'd reverse?

hook: Short-term pays today — long-term pays after you're fired.
plain: CMOs know they should invest in brand-building and long-term equity. But they put all their budget into short-term performance marketing because they don't believe they'll be in the role long enough to see the payoff. The system punishes patient thinking — so nobody does it.
analogy: The Hollywood exec gets fired after two bad years. The next guy makes a fortune off films the first exec greenlit. The person who plants the tree never eats the fruit.
prompt: What's one brand-building investment you've been avoiding because you won't be around to claim credit when it pays off? What would need to change for you to make it anyway?

hook: People fear missing out more than they value gaining.
plain: Loss aversion is the psychological principle that losing something feels worse than gaining the same thing feels good. In marketing, highlighting what someone won't get if they choose a competitor can be more powerful than listing what they will get if they choose you.
analogy: A USP doesn't just say 'we have X' — it says 'if you don't buy from us, you lose X'. The fear of missing that one feature drives the decision more than the promise of having it.
prompt: Think of your core offer. What specific outcome do people fail to get if they don't work with you — stated as a loss, not just an absence of gain?

hook: People hate being told what to do — reframe it as a trade and resistance disappears.
plain: An imposition feels like control; an exchange feels like fairness. Governments and companies impose rules that trigger backlash, but if the same rule is presented as 'we're giving you X in return for Y,' compliance rises. The policy doesn't change — only the framing. People tolerate constraints when they see what they get in return.
analogy: A city introduces 20 mph speed limits and drivers revolt. Reframe it: '20 mph limit, but we're removing all speed bumps.' Same rule, framed as a deal — acceptance jumps.
prompt: What policy, rule, or constraint in your organization is framed as a mandate that could be reframed as a fair exchange?

hook: Small unexpected features multiply loyalty — at low cost.
plain: Easter eggs are surprising, low-cost product details that create disproportionate delight. A toaster with a 'just a little bit more' button doesn't cost much to add, but it makes you want to use that brand forever. These features work because they exceed expectations in a specific, memorable way.
analogy: A Mercedes seat that inflates around corners makes you feel like you're on a mission to Mars. It doesn't make the car faster, but it makes you choose to drive it every single time.
prompt: What's the smallest detail you could add to your product, service, or workflow that would make someone say 'I wasn't expecting that — that's brilliant'?

hook: Insisting everything make sense in advance restricts you to 15% of possible solutions.
plain: If you demand that every step of a process be logically justified before you take it, you can only explore the part of the solution space that's already obvious. The other 85% of potential solutions require lateral leaps that don't make sense until after you discover them. Penicillin, Viagra, and the theory of evolution were all found backward — discovered first, rationalized later. Creativity requires tolerating steps that don't make sense yet.
analogy: A team brainstorms product ideas but rejects anything that doesn't fit the current business model. They miss the breakthrough that would've created a new category because it required an irrational leap.
prompt: What idea in your business has been rejected because no one could justify it in advance, and what would happen if you tested it anyway?

hook: Shift payment from upfront cost to shared savings — removes buyer's risk entirely.
plain: Instead of selling a product for a fixed price, provide it for free and charge a percentage of the measurable benefit it creates. Matthew Boulton and James Watt did this in 1775 with steam engines — charging 50% of the coal savings instead of asking mines to buy the machine upfront. Removes the buyer's uncertainty about whether the product will deliver value.
analogy: A solar panel company installs panels on your roof at no charge and takes 30% of your reduced electricity bills. You only pay if it works — zero risk, immediate adoption.
prompt: What product or service in your business could you provide now and charge for only after it delivers measurable value?

hook: More ways to buy means more people buy — forcing one channel optimizes cost, kills revenue.
plain: Customers have different preferences for how they want to transact — some want to call, some want to click, some want to walk in. Offering multiple channels increases total sales because it removes friction for each preference type. Finance teams push everyone toward the lowest-cost channel, which saves pennies but loses pounds by excluding customers who need a different path.
analogy: A hotel removes the front desk to save labor, forcing all check-ins through a kiosk. Business travelers love it; elderly guests and non-English speakers leave and book elsewhere.
prompt: Which buying channel did your business eliminate in the name of efficiency, and what customer segment quietly disappeared with it?

hook: Performance marketing measures what happened after the ad, not because of it.
plain: Modern digital advertising platforms excel at detecting when someone was exposed to an ad before making a purchase, then claiming credit for the sale. But correlation isn't causation. If someone visits a Jeep dealership and then sees a Jeep ad online, the platform will report the ad 'drove' the sale — even though the person was already in-market. The system is designed to take credit, not prove effectiveness.
analogy: You decide to buy running shoes, Google it, click an ad, and purchase. The ad platform reports a conversion. But you were buying those shoes anyway — the ad was just in the path.
prompt: If you removed all digital retargeting for one month, which 'conversions' would still happen on their own because the customer was already deciding to buy?

hook: Lateral thinking gets you under the hump to a higher peak — logic keeps you trapped.
plain: In complexity theory, a 'local maximum' is a decent solution that traps you because every logical next step makes things worse. Creativity is the process of taking an irrational leap that looks wrong but lands you in a better place. If everyone defines a problem the same way, you're stuck on a local maximum. Lateral thinking lets you escape to a higher solution that sequential logic could never reach.
analogy: A company optimizes its website checkout flow for years, squeezing out 2% gains. Then someone says 'let people buy without creating an account' — conversion doubles because they left the local maximum.
prompt: What problem in your business is 'fully optimized' but still mediocre, and what irrational change might land you on a higher peak?

hook: We don't know why we buy things — social copying is invisible to us.
plain: When asked why you bought something, you'll construct a plausible-sounding explanation. But much of purchase behavior is driven by forces you're unaware of — seeing a friend buy something first, wanting to fit in, avoiding the discomfort of being first. Social copying is one of the strongest drivers of adoption, yet most people never realize it influenced them. You think you decided rationally; you actually copied your brother.
analogy: You buy an electric car and say it's because of fuel savings and the environment. But you didn't research EVs until your colleague bought one and you saw it worked. The real reason: social proof.
prompt: Think of the last major purchase you made and reverse-engineer it: who bought one first, and would you have considered it if they hadn't?

hook: Charging 30% more beats doubling volume — margins are everything, but marketers chase users.
plain: In most categories, getting existing customers to pay 30% more is far more profitable than doubling the number of customers. Margins are thin, so volume growth is expensive and often unprofitable at scale. But marketers and growth teams obsess over acquisition and conversion, ignoring the fact that perception and brand equity can justify higher prices with zero incremental cost.
analogy: A software company spends $2 million acquiring twice as many users at the current price and loses money. They raise prices 25%, lose 10% of users, and profit triples.
prompt: If you raised prices by 30% tomorrow and lost 20% of your customers, would you still be more profitable — and if yes, why aren't you testing it?

hook: Billing by the hour rewards slow work — not valuable work.
plain: When you bill by the hour, you make more money by taking longer. This creates a perverse incentive where speed and insight are punished, and time-consuming busy-work is rewarded. The best ideas take 10 minutes — but you can't charge for that under an hourly model.
analogy: A lawyer pulling in a million dollars a year says, 'I spend three times as long justifying what we charged as doing the actual legal work.' The incentive structure rewards documentation, not results.
prompt: If you were paid for outcomes instead of hours, what's the first thing you'd stop doing?

hook: Account people fear going behind the client's back — so they never reach the real decision-maker.
plain: Agency account managers depend on a single client relationship for their entire career, which makes them terrified of doing anything that might look like circumventing their main contact. They won't pitch ideas to the CEO or CFO, even when those people control the real budget, because it feels like betrayal. This relationship paranoia keeps agencies trapped in low-stakes marcom work instead of influencing business strategy.
analogy: An agency has a breakthrough idea for restructuring a client's pricing model, but the account lead won't present it to the CFO because it would 'go over the CMO's head' — so the idea dies.
prompt: What senior decision-maker in your client's organization would benefit from your thinking, but you've never approached them because it feels politically risky?
